               Employment and Earnings is prepared by the
           Division of Industry Employment Statistics and the
           Division of Employment and Unemployment Anal-
           ysis in collaboration with the Division of Special
           Publications. The data are collected by the Bureau
           of the Census (Department of Commerce), State
           Employment Security Agencies, and State Depart-
           ments of Labor in cooperation with the Bureau of
           Labor Statistics. A brief description of the co-
           operative statistical programs of the BLS with
           these agencies is presented in the Explanatory
           Notes. The State agencies are listed on the inside
           back cover.                                                                        CALENDAR OF SPECIAL FEATURES

                            Employment and Unemployment Developments,
                                             May 1978

          ESTABLISHMENT DATA
                                                                   Seasonal Factors for Employment Data for Women
                     The following table presents seasonal adjustment factors for all women worker series in the establishment section of this periodical. These factors were derived using data through February 1978. The
               factors should be used with current estimates (adjusted to the March 1974 benchmarks) beginning September 1977 for all series shown in the following table.
                     The seasonal movements are measured in order to adjust the data statistically for such recurring events as warm and cold weather, crop-growing cycles, holidays, vacations, regular industry model change-
               over periods, and the like. These movements are generally the largest single component of month-to-month change in employment, hours, earnings, and labor turnover. The seasonal factors which follow
               enable the analyst to remove these influences from the data in order to determine more basic trends.




          2. Seasonal adjustment factors for women employees on nonagricultural payrolls,
          by industry division and major manufacturing group

                                                                     Explanatory Notes

                                These explanatory notes provide information on the concepts, methodology, and scope of Household Data
                                (A tables), Establishment Data (8, C, and D tables), and State and Area Unemployment Data (E table) pub-
                                lished in Employment and Earnings.



                                                                             Introduction

                 The statistics in this periodical are compiled from two major            Employment
              sources: (1) Household interviews, and (2) reports from employers.

                in the "other" population.

                                                                                         were made in order to improve the efficiency of the sample

                Labor turnover                                                          ployees are excluded from the employment estimates if the work
                   Labor turnover is the gross movement of wage and salary work-        stoppage extends through the report period.
               ers into and out of employed status with respect to individual
               establishments. This movement, which relates to a calendar month,
               is divided into two broad types: Accessions (new hires and rehires)      ESTIMATING METHODS
               and separations (terminations of employment initiated by either
               employer or employee). Each type of action is cumulated for a                The principal features of the procedure used to estimate em-
               calendar month and expressed as a rate per 100 employees. The            ployment for the industry statistics are (1) the use of the "link
               date relate to all employees, whether full- or part-time, permanent      relative" technique, which is a form of ratio estimation, (2) periodic
               or temporary, including executive, office, sales, other salaried         adjustment of employment levels to new benchmarks, and (3) the
               personnel, and production workers. Transfers to another establish-       use of size and regional stratification.
               ment of the company are included, beginning with January 1959.
                   Accessions are the total number of permanent and temporary
               additions to the employment roll, including both new and rehired
               employees.                                                               The " l i n k relative" technique

                                                                                            From a sample composed of establishments reporting for both
                   New hires are temporary or permanent additions to the em-            the previous and current months, the ratio of current month em-
                ployment roll of persons who have never before been employed in         ployment to that of the previous month is computed. This is

                                                                       Seasonal adjustment

                    Many economic statistics reflect a regularly recurring seasonal         The seasonal adjustment methods used for these series are an
                movement which can be estimated on the basis of past experience.         adaptation of the standard ratio-to-moving average method, with a
                By eliminating that part of the change which can be ascribed to          provision for moving "adjustment factors" to take account of
                usual seasonal variation, it is possible to observe the cyclical and     changing seasonal patterns. A detailed description of the method
